text: The surgeon retracts the spleen and divides the gastroesplenic ligament. The splenocolic ligament is dissected and divided, followed by the dissection of the splenorenal and phrenosplenic ligaments. The spleen is then elevated medially.

The surgeon identifies the tail of the pancreas and dissects it using sharp instruments. The short gastric vessels are identified and ligated using bipolar energy, facilitating the mobilization of surrounding ligaments.

The surgeon focuses on the tail of the pancreas, performing careful sharp and blunt dissection to detach it from the splenic hilum while avoiding injury to the pancreatic capsule. The splenic hilum is meticulously dissected, and branches of the splenic vein are identified, dissected, and individually ligated.

The tail of the pancreas is shown densely attached to surrounding structures. The surgeon carefully dissects the hyaline free, mobilizes the pancreas, and uses a GIA vascular stapler to transect the hyaline.
The specimen is placed into a fifteen millimeter retrieval bag. The surgical area is inspected for any bleeding, and hemostasis is achieved.
